input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin was built using a model‑based approach that integrates SysML for requirements and architecture definition, component–connector ADLs such as MontiArc (with its Automaton extension) and SysML Interaction‑Block Diagrams for logical architecture, and MathWorks Simulink as a simulation engine for dynamic behavior. The behavioral models are compiled into executable code written in C++ or Java, which is then assembled into a runtime environment that processes data from the physical system and stores results in a relational database for later analysis.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Digital Twin computational models are defined using SysML for hierarchical architectural representation (requirements, functional, logical, product/technical layers), MontiArc for component‑connector modeling and state‑machine behavior, and Simulink for detailed behavioral simulation. The constellation comprises geometric, behavioral and analytical models linked across abstraction levels; data objects include simulation outputs and embedded measurement data exchanged through C++/Java code. Models are managed in MBSE workflows with model libraries, versioning and simulation repositories.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The DT lifecycle follows a structured, MBSE‑driven sequence: 1) Requirements modelling in SysML, captured via UML requirement diagrams; 2) Functional architecture defined with SysML IBDs or ACME, detailing component interfaces and connectors; 3) Logical architecture modelled with MontiArc Automaton or AADL, incorporating state‑machine behaviour; 4) Product/technical layer realised through MathWorks Simulink simulation blocks translated to executable C++ or Java code. Transition between stages is achieved through model transformation (e.g., SysML to AADL) and round‑trip engineering. Governance is guided by formalised MBSE methodologies such as RFLP or SMArDT, which prescribe versioning, change‑impact analysis, and lifecycle checkpoints.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process combines MBSE, ADL, and simulation to evolve the DT. Engineering starts with SysML requirement capture, then functional/architectural refinement using SysML IBDs or MontiArc, followed by behavioural simulation in MathWorks Simulink to validate logical consistency. Code generation targets C++/Java implementations, which are version‑controlled (e.g., Git). QA and validation use simulation‑based unit tests and model‑in‑the‑loop (MiTL) against sensor data. The SMArDT methodology is employed to manage evolution: a schema of change‑log, hierarchical versioning of artefacts, and rollback capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: As described in [52], Mosquitto implements MQTT authentication via user/password or X.509 client certificates, along with TLSv1.2/1.3 for transport encryption and support for anonymous access. In [53], Thingsboard uses role‑based access control, JWT‑based device authentication, TLS for MQTT and HTTPS, and fine‑grained permissions on dashboards, assets, and telemetry. The RAMI 4.0 reference model (see [48], [49]) situates security as a cross‑cutting layer, recommending adherence to IEC 62443, OPC UA secure channel and AuthC protocols, and the use of threat‑risk assessment matrices. Together, these mechanisms provide authentication, authorization, secure communication, threat mitigation, and fail‑safe configuration for the digital twin system.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.
